description: Small labeled eval set for filtering-score behavior. Each item has synthetic-but-representative content and an expected coarse band (high/strong/moderate/weak/reject). Used by scripts/eval_filtering.py to measure determinism, grid adherence, and band alignment. Expected bands were calibrated against a live Sonnet 4.6 run (2026-06, thinking OFF): the tuned rubric is stricter than first-guess labels (e.g. a pure vendor click-through tutorial scores 'reject', a no-novelty educational recap scores 'weak'). Extend with real article excerpts as needed.
included_topics: Foundation Model Releases (e.g., GPT, Claude, Gemini, Grok)
Major Product/Service/Feature Launches Using Foundation Models

articles.title: Introducing Claude 4.6: A New Frontier Foundation Model
articles.content: Today we release Claude 4.6, a new foundation model. This post is a primary, in-depth account of the release: the model's architecture (a sparse mixture-of-experts transformer with 64 routed experts), the training recipe (a three-stage curriculum of pretraining, mid-training on synthetic reasoning traces, and RLHF), and benchmark results across MMLU, GPQA, and SWE-bench. We detail the new 1M-token context window, the adaptive-thinking mechanism that dynamically allocates reasoning tokens, and the inference-time routing that cuts cost by 40%. Foundation model releases like this are the core focus throughout. We include ablations showing each training stage's contribution, latency/throughput curves, and a discussion of safety evaluations. The release is the primary subject of the entire article, covered with expert depth and novel architectural detail.

articles.title: A Rigorous Empirical Study of Retrieval-Augmented Generation Latency
articles.content: We present a careful empirical study of latency in retrieval-augmented generation systems. Across 12 configurations we measure end-to-end latency, recall@k, and answer faithfulness, with statistical significance testing and confidence intervals. The work offers a clear, well-validated incremental contribution: a caching strategy for vector lookups that reduces p99 latency by 30% with no measurable recall loss. We compare against three baselines, provide reproducible code, and analyze failure cases. The study is solid and insightful though not groundbreaking; it is a strong comparative evaluation rather than a novel theoretical advance.

articles.title: Understanding Attention: An Educational Walkthrough
articles.content: This educational post explains the attention mechanism for readers new to transformers. It walks through query-key-value projections, scaled dot-product attention, and multi-head attention with worked numeric examples and diagrams. The content is clear and pedagogically useful with some fresh framing, but it covers well-established material and offers limited novelty. It is implementation-and-explanation focused with modest research context, suitable as a learning resource rather than a research contribution.

articles.title: How to Deploy a Model on Our Platform in 5 Steps
articles.content: This tutorial shows how to click through our cloud console to deploy a pretrained model endpoint. Step 1: create an account. Step 2: upload your model artifact. Step 3: pick an instance type. Step 4: click deploy. Step 5: call the REST endpoint. The post is a basic vendor-specific how-to with minimal machine-learning insight; it does not discuss model internals, training, or evaluation. It is mostly platform navigation with superficial ML coverage.

articles.title: Why Our Company Culture Makes Us Great
articles.content: Our company was founded on the belief that people come first. In this post our CEO shares the story of our annual offsite retreat, our commitment to work-life balance, and the new espresso machines in the break room. We also announce our updated brand colors and a redesigned logo. There is no machine-learning, research, or technical content here; this is a promotional culture-and-marketing piece.

articles.title: Deep Learning for Protein Folding in Drug Discovery
articles.content: This article applies deep learning to protein structure prediction for pharmaceutical drug discovery, a healthcare and biology application. While it uses neural networks, the entire focus is on biological molecular dynamics, wet-lab validation of candidate compounds, and clinical relevance — squarely within the excluded Biology and Healthcare topics. The ML method is incidental to a biology/healthcare subject that is explicitly excluded.
